request.question: Which Canadian singer sang " something stronger" in the musical applause?

title: Sweden in the Eurovision Song Contest 1965
Applause, Applause
The Singer Sang His Song
Jumbo (Bee Gees song)
Applause (Bonnie Franklin song)
Anna Russell
Spin It Again! Live in Munich
Leland Palmer (actress)
Jennifer Gillis
Len Cariou
sentences: After a year of absence, Sweden competed in the Eurovision Song Contest 1965 with the opera singer Ingvar Wixell, and the song "Absent Friend" (originally "Annorstädes vals") composed by Dag Wirén.
For the first time, one singer sang all the songs in the national final, and Ingvar was also the first male singer to represent Sweden.
It was the first ESC entry that was not performed in the country's native language; this led to the introduction of the language rule the following year.
Applause, Applause was a Canadian musical variety television miniseries which aired on CBC Television in 1974.
"The Singer Sang His Song" is a song written by Barry, Robin and Maurice Gibb and recorded by the English rock group Bee Gees, released in early 1968 as a single along with Jumbo.
In some countries the song was the B-side of Jumbo but in others they were promoted as a double A-side.
"Jumbo" is a song released by the Bee Gees, written by Barry, Robin & Maurice Gibb.
It was released as a double A side with "The Singer Sang His Song" but featured as the lead track in some territories.

"Applause" is the title song from the 1970 Broadway musical "Applause", with music by Charles Strouse and lyrics by Lee Adams, originally performed by Bonnie Franklin, who originated the role of Bonnie in the musical, and recorded as a single with orchestra and chorus conducted by Donald Pippin.
The single was released with a B-side featuring the star of the production, Lauren Bacall, making her musical theatre debut, performing "Something Greater" together with Len Cariou.

The single's popularity led to Franklin's being invited to perform it on the 24th Tony Awards broadcast on television, where the show gained Best Musical, Bacall Best Leading Actress in a Musical, but Bonnie Franklin missed out on the best supporting actress to Melba Moore.
Anna Russell (born Anna Claudia Russell-Brown; 27 December 191118 October 2006) was an English–Canadian singer and comedian.
She gave many concerts in which she sang and played comic musical sketches on the piano.

Among her best-known works are her concert performances and famous recordings of "The Ring of the Nibelungs (An Analysis)" – a humorous 22-minute synopsis of Richard Wagner's "Der Ring des Nibelungen" – and (on the same album) her parody "How to Write Your Own Gilbert and Sullivan Opera."
Spin It Again!
Live in Munich is a live album and video by the Canadian band Saga.
It was recorded in 2012 at the end of their 20/20 tour and celebrates the reunion with original singer Michael Sadler.
It was also the first Saga release featuring their new drummer Mike Thorne.
Besides three songs from "20/20", it includes mainly songs from the first five Saga albums.
The concert was released on different formats by edel records/earMUSIC, including a double CD, DVD (Super Jewel Box packaging) and Blu-Ray.
The video formats also include a twenty-minute tour documentary.
The CD has edited applause between the main set and the encores, but otherwise the performance is identical.
As is typical for Saga shows in Germany, Michael Sadler does some German announcements.
The concert also includes a litte "unplugged" set.
After returning to the stage, Sadler says "What shall I play?"
He starts on "Images (Chapter 1)", then stops, goes into "No Regrets (Chapter 5)", stops again and finally declares "I got it!"
before launching into "Tired World (Chapter 6)".
Leland Palmer (born June 16, 1945, Port Washington, New York) is an American actress, dancer, and singer who has appeared on stage, in motion pictures, and on television.
She appeared on Broadway in "Bajour", "A Joyful Noise", "Hello, Dolly!
", "Applause", and "Pippin".
Palmer received two Tony Award nominations: in 1967 for featured actress in a musical (Miss Jimmie in "A Joyful Noise"), and in 1973 for actress in a musical (Fastrada in "Pippin").
She appeared as Viola in the rock musical "Your Own Thing" during a highly regarded run at the Huntington Hartford Theater in Los Angeles in August 1968.

Jennifer Kristine Gillis (born 26 April 1996) is a Canadian singer, dancer and actress with an extensive resume in musical theatre, television, singing, radio, recording, and animated voice-over work.

Gillis is most noted for performing in Sir Andrew Lloyd Webber's CBC TV reality show "Over the Rainbow"-a competition to be cast as the leading role of Dorothy Gale in Lloyd Webber's forthcoming production of "The Wizard of Oz" in Toronto, Ontario.
Being the youngest aspiring singer in the competition, she singularly represented her province of British Columbia and as a result was named the Top 6th musical theatre performer in all of Canada.
Since "Over the Rainbow", Gillis sang the Canadian national anthem "O Canada" for the Prime Minister of Canada, Stephen Harper on Parliament Hill in Ottawa, Ontario on Canada Day in 2013.
She aspires to become a Broadway actress someday and wants to dedicate her life to performing.
Leonard Joseph "Len" Cariou (born September 30, 1939) is a Canadian actor, best known for his portrayal of Sweeney Todd in the original cast of "", which earned him a Tony award.
He currently plays the patriarch, Henry Reagan, NYPD Police Commissioner (retired), in the multi-generational television series "Blue Bloods" on CBS.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- If summary_1 already includes explicit profession/role/descriptor tokens for an anchor (for example, 'singer', 'director', 'country music artist'), omit those redundant descriptor tokens and emit a compact anchor-only query consisting of the core visible anchors (names, titles, groups). Retain relation tokens only when the question explicitly requests them or when disambiguation rules demand them. Do not remove descriptors when the question explicitly asks for role/occupation evidence or when a short-title disambiguation rule requires adding an entertainment disambiguator.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity (or otherwise names candidate/bridge entities), treat the context claim as the summary anchor and perform explicit identity-alignment checks before using passage facts. For each entity explicitly named in `context` as a candidate or bridge, do the following: if at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append a single brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that linking fact and references the corroborating passage and also include one short clause for that context-named entity giving the passage-supported relation value (e.g., "Name: [relation value]."); if no passage provides the relation value for that context-named entity, append a short clause stating that no relation value is provided in the evidence (e.g., "Name: no [relation] provided in evidence."). Do not create such clauses for entities that are only mentioned in `passages` and are not named in `context`. Do not append corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep these additions short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.
- Vocal-role single-token normalization (narrow scope): If summary_2 (alone or composed with summary_1 where identity links are explicit) explicitly contains vocal-role evidence — i.e., one or more verbalized vocal-role phrases that clearly denote a singing performer (examples: "lead singer", "lead vocals", "vocalist" as presented in the summaries) — then you may prefer to emit the concise single-token common noun for a person who sings (e.g., "singer") only when that single-token common noun itself appears verbatim in the summaries or when the vocal-role phrases in the summaries unambiguously denote the same person-role. If those explicit lexical licenses are not present, retain the literal role phrasing as given in summary_2 and do not normalize modifiers or drop qualifiers.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Which Canadian singer sang " something stronger" in the musical applause?
hop1_docs: Applause, Applause | Applause, Applause was a Canadian musical variety television miniseries which aired on CBC Television in 1974.

Applause (Bonnie Franklin song) | "Applause" is the title song from the 1970 Broadway musical "Applause", with music by Charles Strouse and lyrics by Lee Adams, originally performed by Bonnie Franklin, who originated the role of Bonnie in the musical, and recorded as a single with orchestra and chorus conducted by Donald Pippin.  The single was released with a B-side featuring the star of the production, Lauren Bacall, making her musical theatre debut, performing "Something Greater" together with Len Cariou.  The single's popularity led to Franklin's being invited to perform it on the 24th Tony Awards broadcast on television, where the show gained Best Musical, Bacall Best Leading Actress in a Musical, but Bonnie Franklin missed out on the best supporting actress to Melba Moore.

Siti binti Saad | Siti binti Saad (1880–1950) was a pioneering artist in the taraab genre of east African music.  In an era in which male singers predominated, she was a pioneer as a woman singer in the genre: she was the first woman in East Africa to record her music in an album.  In contrast to previous singers who sang in Arabic, she sang in Swahili.  She sang in cities of the coast of Tanganyika and Zanzibar.

Parichay (singer) | Parichay (born August 22) is an Indo-Canadian singer, composer, and music producer.  He has released three albums and collaborated in Bollywood film songs including a remix of "Yamla Pagla Deewana" and "O Meri Chandni" from the film "Chaar Din Ki Chandni" which he sang with Sunidhi Chauhan.  In 2016, he composed music for the Bollywood film "Loveshhuda".

Applause Cheer Boo Hiss | Applause Cheer Boo Hiss is an EP by Canadian indie rock band Land of Talk, released on April 4, 2006 on Dependent Music in Canada.  It would later be released on Rebel Group Records in the United States on March 20, 2007.

Applause (musical) | Applause is a musical with a book by Betty Comden and Adolph Green, lyrics by Lee Adams, and music by Charles Strouse.  The musical is based on the 1950 film "All About Eve" and the short story on which the movie is based, Mary Orr's "The Wisdom of Eve".  The story centers on aging star Margo Channing, who innocently takes a fledgling actress under her wing, unaware that the ruthless Eve is plotting to steal her career and her man.

Stronger (Carlene Carter album) | Stronger is an album released in 2008 by American country music singer Carlene Carter.  Included is "I'm So Cool," a song originally recorded on Carter's 1980 album, "Musical Shapes".
response.summary_1: Passage [2] states that the Applause single "was released with a B-side featuring the star of the production, Lauren Bacall ... performing 'Something Greater' together with Len Cariou." Bridge entity: Len Cariou. Missing / needed for next hop: (1) confirmation of Len Cariou's nationality (passages do not state whether he is Canadian) and (2) confirmation that the song title is "Something Greater" (the user wrote "something stronger"). Retrieval direction / anchors: fetch Len Cariou's biography or the Applause single/discography using anchors such as the quoted phrase "performing 'Something Greater' together with Len Cariou", plus queries like "Len Cariou nationality" and "Len Cariou 'Something Greater' Applause B-side Lauren Bacall".
response.hop2_query: Len Cariou nationality Something Greater Lauren Bacall Applause B-side
hop2_docs: Applause (Bonnie Franklin song) | "Applause" is the title song from the 1970 Broadway musical "Applause", with music by Charles Strouse and lyrics by Lee Adams, originally performed by Bonnie Franklin, who originated the role of Bonnie in the musical, and recorded as a single with orchestra and chorus conducted by Donald Pippin.  The single was released with a B-side featuring the star of the production, Lauren Bacall, making her musical theatre debut, performing "Something Greater" together with Len Cariou.  The single's popularity led to Franklin's being invited to perform it on the 24th Tony Awards broadcast on television, where the show gained Best Musical, Bacall Best Leading Actress in a Musical, but Bonnie Franklin missed out on the best supporting actress to Melba Moore.

Len Cariou | Leonard Joseph "Len" Cariou (born September 30, 1939) is a Canadian actor, best known for his portrayal of Sweeney Todd in the original cast of "", which earned him a Tony award.  He currently plays the patriarch, Henry Reagan, NYPD Police Commissioner (retired), in the multi-generational television series "Blue Bloods" on CBS.

Bacall to Arms | Bacall to Arms is a 1946 Warner Bros. cartoon in the Merrie Melodies series, directed by Bob Clampett, in his second-to-last cartoon at Warner Bros. ("The Big Snooze" would be the last).  Neither Clampett (he was left uncredited because he had left the studio before the cartoon was released) nor voice characterizations are credited.  Mel Blanc's voice is recognizable as a fat theater patron, a husband in a newsreel, and the wolf's vocal effects.  Impressionist Dave Barry portrays the voice of Humphrey Bogart while June Foray voices Lauren Bacall.  The title refers both to Hemingway's "A Farewell to Arms" and actress Lauren Bacall, whose acclaimed film debut was in "To Have and Have Not", based on another Hemingway novel, as well as a play on the term "A Call to Arms".

Lauren Bacall on screen and stage | American actress Lauren Bacall (1924–2014) had an extensive career in films, television shows, and plays.  She was one of the leading ladies during the Golden Age of Hollywood along with actresses such as Marilyn Monroe and Rita Hayworth.  Bacall started her career as a teenage fashion model when she appeared on the cover of "Harper's Bazaar" and was discovered by Howard Hawks' wife Nancy.  As she naturally had a high-pitched and nasal voice, she received lessons to help deepen it and was required to shout verses by Shakespeare for hours every day as part of her training.

Lauren Bacall | Lauren Bacall ( , born Betty Joan Perske; September 16, 1924 – August 12, 2014) was an American actress and singer known for her distinctive voice and sultry looks.  She was named the 20th greatest female star of Classic Hollywood cinema by the American Film Institute, and received an Academy Honorary Award from the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences in 2009, "in recognition of her central place in the Golden Age of motion pictures."

How Little We Know | "How Little We Know" is a song written by written by Hoagy Carmichael and Johnny Mercer for the 1944 film "To Have and Have Not", where it is performed by the character "Slim" played by Lauren Bacall.  A young Andy Williams recorded the song for the film as a possible alternative track to dub Bacall's low voice.  Bacall said they used her singing.  After the film's release it was a hit recording sung by Judy Johnson.

Dark Passage (film) | Dark Passage (1947) is a Warner Bros. film noir directed by Delmer Daves and starring Humphrey Bogart and Lauren Bacall.  The film is based on the novel of the same name by David Goodis.  It was the third of four films real-life couple Bacall and Bogart made together.
hop2_titles: Lauren Bacall on screen and stage
response.summary_2: The Applause single's B-side featured Lauren Bacall performing "Something Greater" together with Len Cariou (passage [1]) — the title is "Something Greater," not "Something Stronger." Len Cariou: Canadian (passage [2]).
